--- title: "Bikeshare_SpaceTime_Prediction" subtitle: "CPLN 5080 - Spring 2026" author: "Alejandro Duque" date: today format: html: code-fold: show code-tools: true toc: true toc-depth: 3 toc-location: left theme: cosmo embed-resources: true editor: visual execute: warning: false message: false --- This report analyzes Q3 2024 Indego bike share ridership in Philadelphia using space-time prediction models. Using trip data from July through September 2024, we build a series of progressively complex regression models to predict hourly demand at each station, evaluate spatial and temporal error patterns, and reflect on the equity implications of deploying such a system. # Priming my Code ##Setting up the Code ##Plot Priming #Part 1: Importing and Prepping the Data ##Loading in the Indego Data ##Examining The Data Structure ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # How many trips? cat("Total trips in Q3 2024:", nrow(indego), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Total trips in Q3 2024: 408408 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Date range cat("Date range:", min(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "to", max(mdy_hm(indego$start_time)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Date range: 1719792120 to 1727740740 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # How many unique stations? cat("Unique start stations:", length(unique(indego$start_station)), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Unique start stations: 261 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Trip types table(indego$trip_route_category) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` One Way Round Trip 380939 27469 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Passholder types table(indego$passholder_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Day Pass Indego30 Indego365 Walk-up 22885 239857 132358 13308 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} # Bike types table(indego$bike_type) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` electric standard 236839 171569 ``` ::: ::: ## Creating Time Bins ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} indego <- indego %>% mutate( # Parse datetime start_datetime = parse_date_time(start_time, orders = "m/d/Y H:M"), end_datetime = parse_date_time(end_time, orders = "m/d/Y H:M"), # Create hourly bins interval60 = floor_date(start_datetime, unit = "hour"), # Extract time features week = week(interval60), month = month(interval60, label = TRUE), dotw = wday(interval60, label = TRUE), hour = hour(interval60), date = as.Date(interval60), # Create useful indicators weekend = ifelse(dotw %in% c("Sat", "Sun"), 1, 0), rush_hour = ifelse(hour %in% c(7, 8, 9, 16, 17, 18), 1, 0) ) # Look at temporal features head(indego %>% select(start_datetime, interval60, week, dotw, hour, weekend)) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` # A tibble: 6 × 6 start_datetime interval60 week dotw hour weekend 1 2024-07-01 00:02:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 2 2024-07-01 00:03:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 3 2024-07-01 00:04:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 4 2024-07-01 00:05:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 5 2024-07-01 00:06:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 6 2024-07-01 00:06:00 2024-07-01 00:00:00 27 Mon 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ##Trips over Time ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Daily trip counts daily_trips <- indego %>% group_by(date) %>% summarize(trips = n()) ggplot(daily_trips, aes(x = date, y = trips)) + geom_line(color = "#3182bd", linewidth = 1) + geom_smooth(se = FALSE, color = "red", linetype = "dashed") + labs( title = "Indego Daily Ridership - Q3 2024", subtitle = "Late summer demand patterns in Philadelphia", x = "Date", y = "Daily Trips", caption = "Source: Indego bike share" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/trips_over_time-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ##Hourly Trips ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Average trips by hour and day type hourly_patterns <- indego %>% group_by(hour, weekend) %>% summarize(avg_trips = n() / n_distinct(date)) %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) ggplot(hourly_patterns, aes(x = hour, y = avg_trips, color = day_type)) + geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) + scale_color_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) + labs( title = "Average Hourly Ridership Patterns", subtitle = "Clear commute patterns on weekdays", x = "Hour of Day", y = "Average Trips per Hour", color = "Day Type" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-1-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ##Top Stations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Most popular origin stations top_stations <- indego %>% count(start_station, start_lat, start_lon, name = "trips") %>% arrange(desc(trips)) %>% head(20) kable(top_stations, caption = "Top 20 Indego Stations by Trip Origins", format.args = list(big.mark = ",")) %>% kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped", "hover")) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} `````{=html}

+ mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/map_philly-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: ## Joining Census Data to Stations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Create sf object for stations stations_sf <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% st_as_sf(coords = c("start_lon", "start_lat"), crs = 4326) # Spatial join to get census tract for each station stations_census <- st_join(stations_sf, philly_census, left = TRUE) %>% st_drop_geometry() # Look at the result - investigate whether all of the stations joined to census data -- according to the map above there are stations in non-residential tracts. stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Add back to trip data indego_census <- indego %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White, Total_Pop), by = "start_station" ) # Prepare data for visualization stations_for_map <- indego %>% distinct(start_station, start_lat, start_lon) %>% filter(!is.na(start_lat), !is.na(start_lon)) %>% left_join( stations_census %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc), by = "start_station" ) %>% mutate(has_census = !is.na(Med_Inc)) # Create the map showing problem stations ggplot() + geom_sf(data = philly_census, aes(fill = Med_Inc), color = "white", size = 0.1) + scale_fill_viridis( option = "viridis", name = "Median\nIncome", labels = scales::dollar, na.value = "grey90" ) + # Stations with census data (small grey dots) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "grey30", size = 1, alpha = 0.6 ) + # Stations WITHOUT census data (red X marks the spot) geom_point( data = stations_for_map %>% filter(!has_census), aes(x = start_lon, y = start_lat), color = "red", size = 1, shape = 4, stroke = 1.5 ) + labs( title = "Philadelphia Median Household Income by Census Tract", subtitle = "Indego stations shown (RED = no census data match)", caption = "Red X marks indicate stations that didn't join to census tracts" ) + mapTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} Holiday indicator for Q3 holidays is_holiday = case_when( month(interval60) == 7 & day(interval60) == 4 ~ 1, # July 4th month(interval60) == 9 & wday(interval60) == 2 & day(interval60) <= 7 ~ 1, # Labor Day (first Monday in Sept) TRUE ~ 0 ), #Perfect biking weather (65-80°F, no rain) perfect_weather = ifelse( Temperature >= 65 & Temperature <= 80 & Precipitation == 0, 1, 0 ), #Too hot indicator (>88°F suppresses ridership in summer) too_hot = ifelse(Temperature > 88, 1, 0) ) ``` ::: # Creating Temporal Lag Variables ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sort by station and time study_panel <- study_panel %>% arrange(start_station, interval60) # Create lag variables WITHIN each station study_panel <- study_panel %>% group_by(start_station) %>% mutate( lag1Hour = lag(Trip_Count, 1), lag2Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 2), lag3Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 3), lag12Hours = lag(Trip_Count, 12), lag1day = lag(Trip_Count, 24), ) %>% ungroup() # Remove rows with NA lags (first 24 hours for each station) study_panel_complete <- study_panel %>% filter(!is.na(lag1day)) cat("Rows after removing NA lags:", format(nrow(study_panel_complete), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Rows after removing NA lags: 520,542 ``` ::: ::: ## Visualize Lag Correlations ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Sample one station to visualize example_station <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station == first(start_station)) %>% head(168) # One week # Plot actual vs lagged demand ggplot(example_station, aes(x = interval60)) + geom_line(aes(y = Trip_Count, color = "Current"), linewidth = 1) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1Hour, color = "1 Hour Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + geom_line(aes(y = lag1day, color = "24 Hours Ago"), linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.7) + scale_color_manual(values = c( "Current" = "#08519c", "1 Hour Ago" = "#3182bd", "24 Hours Ago" = "#6baed6" )) + labs( title = "Temporal Lag Patterns at One Station", subtitle = "Past demand predicts future demand", x = "Date-Time", y = "Trip Count", color = "Time Period" ) + plotTheme ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/lag_correlations-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: # Temporal Train / Test Split ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Split by week # Q1 has weeks 1-13 (July-Sept) # Train on weeks 1-9 (July 1 - early Sept) # Test on weeks 10-13 (rest of Sept) # Which stations have trips in BOTH early and late periods? early_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 37) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) late_stations <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 37) %>% distinct(start_station) %>% pull(start_station) # Keep only stations that appear in BOTH periods common_stations <- intersect(early_stations, late_stations) # Filter panel to only common stations study_panel_complete <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(start_station %in% common_stations) # NOW create train/test split train <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week < 37) test <- study_panel_complete %>% filter(week >= 37) cat("Training observations:", format(nrow(train), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training observations: 394,350 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing observations:", format(nrow(test), big.mark = ","), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing observations: 126,192 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Training date range:", format(min(train$date), "%B %d, %Y"), "to", format(max(train$date), "%B %d, %Y"), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Training date range: July 02, 2024 to September 08, 2024 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Testing date range:", format(min(test$date), "%B %d, %Y"), "to", format(max(test$date), "%B %d, %Y"), "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Testing date range: September 09, 2024 to September 30, 2024 ``` ::: ::: #Buidling Predictive Modeling ## Model 1: Baseline (Time + Weather) ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Call: lm(formula = Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + factor(dotw) + Temperature + Precipitation, data = train) Residuals: Min 1Q Median 3Q Max -1.7196 -0.7857 -0.1974 0.1869 23.4317 Coefficients: Estimate Std. In Center City we see high error and high demand, some possible reasons being that the model does not know when event based surges in ridership occur and the unpredictability of tourism surges also plays into the uncertainty. Between July and late September, university students start to move in for the start of the school year which also plays into the factor of unpredictability. ## Temporal Error Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # MAE by time of day and day type temporal_errors <- test %>% group_by(time_of_day, weekend) %>% summarize( MAE = mean(abs_error, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop" ) %>% mutate(day_type = ifelse(weekend == 1, "Weekend", "Weekday")) ggplot(temporal_errors, aes(x = time_of_day, y = MAE, fill = day_type)) + geom_col(position = "dodge") + scale_fill_manual(values = c("Weekday" = "#08519c", "Weekend" = "#6baed6")) + labs( title = "Prediction Errors by Time Period", subtitle = "When is the model struggling most?", x = "Time of Day", y = "Mean Absolute Error (trips)", fill = "Day Type" ) + plotTheme + theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1)) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/temporal_errors-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: We find the highest errors during PM Rush, AM Rush on the weekday, and midday rush. PM and AM rushes have high levels of error, because this is when the model sees the most demand in ridership -- yet, a rainy Tuesday morning, compared to a sunny Friday morning, you will have different ridership numbers, the model does not know this! ## Demographic Error Patterns ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Join demographic data to station errors station_errors_demo <- station_errors %>% left_join( station_attributes %>% select(start_station, Med_Inc, Percent_Taking_Transit, Percent_White), by = "start_station" ) %>% filter(!is.na(Med_Inc)) # Create plots p1 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Med_Inc, y = MAE)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") + scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::dollar) + labs(title = "Errors vs. Median Income", x = "Median Income", y = "MAE") + plotTheme p2 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_Taking_Transit, y = MAE)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") + labs(title = "Errors vs. Transit Usage", x = "% Taking Transit", y = "MAE") + plotTheme p3 <- ggplot(station_errors_demo, aes(x = Percent_White, y = MAE)) + geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#3182bd") + geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "red") + labs(title = "Errors vs. Race", x = "% White", y = "MAE") + plotTheme (p1 | p2) / (p3 | plot_spacer()) ``` ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](duque_lab_5_files/figure-html/errors_demographics-1.png){width=672} ::: ::: According to the model, higher median income neighborhoods are harder to predict for, citing higher levels of MAE. This could be that these wealthier neighborhoods have busier stations, with more unpredictable patterns in ridership. Conversely, when the percentage of residents who take transit increase, the MAE decreases, probably because those neighborhoods who are likely to commute on transit are also more likely to use Indego. Commuting is Predictable! Because wealthier, higher deman neighborhoods also tend to have higher percentages of White residents, MAE in these areas were seen to be high. This makes sense, consistently neighborhoods that are wealthier, have high ridership and more erratic demand patterns that can be sometimes more based on events. Whereas low demand areas are just as hard to predict for. Sparse stations, and lower ridership, make for irregular commuting patterns and the model has less trips to train on. #Feature Engineering & Model Improvement ## Holiday and Good Weather Biking Model ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} model6 <- lm( Trip_Count ~ as.factor(hour) + dotw_simple + Temperature + Precipitation + lag1Hour + lag3Hours + lag1day + Med_Inc + Percent_Taking_Transit + Percent_White + as.factor(start_station) + rush_hour * weekend + is_holiday + perfect_weather + too_hot, data = train ) cat("Model 6 R-squared:", summary(model6)$r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model 6 R-squared: 0.3523554 ``` ::: ```{.r .cell-code} cat("Model 6 Adj R-squared:", summary(model6)$adj.r.squared, "\n") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Model 6 Adj R-squared: 0.3519018 ``` ::: ::: ##Calculating Model 6 MAE and comparing between ::: {.cell} ```{.r .cell-code} # Get predictions on test set # Create day of week factor with treatment (dummy) coding test <- test %>% mutate(dotw_simple = factor(dotw, levels = c("Mon", "Tue", "Wed", "Thu", "Fri", "Sat", "Sun"))) # Set contrasts to treatment coding (dummy variables) contrasts(test$dotw_simple) <- contr.treatment(7) test <- test %>% mutate( pred1 = predict(model1, newdata = test), pred2 = predict(model2, newdata = test), pred3 = predict(model3, newdata = test), pred4 = predict(model4, newdata = test), pred5 = predict(model5, newdata = test), pred6 = predict(model6, newdata = test) ) # Calculate MAE for each model mae_results <- data.frame( Model = c( "1. ````` ::: ::: Two key elements were missing from the previous models, a holiday indicator and a good biking weather indicator. Model 6 aims to include some of these elements in the Q3 2024 Indego data. During Q3, July 4th and Labor Day are two holidays that would break the ridership patterns of typical Philadelphia days. While the perfect weather indicator found hours when temperatures fell between 65 and 85 degrees. Although adding these features are theoretically backed up, they did not improve the model's performance to a substantial degree. In fact, no change in MAE was seen from model 5 to model 6. This is likely due to the fact that the holiday indicator is limited to two days across the whole training data period and even though the perfect weather indicator specified hours, weather was already used in the previous regressions. #Critical Reflection A MAE of 0.70 trips per station-hour sounds impressive on paper, but whether it is "good enough" for Indego to actually use depends heavily on context. For a low demand station averaging 1-2 trips per hour, being off by 0.70 trips is a significant error, that's essentially not knowing whether a bike will be needed at all. For a high demand Center City station averaging 5-6 trips per hour, 0.70 is much more acceptable. So the model's usefulness really depends on which stations Indego is trying to optimize rebalancing for. Prediction errors become most damaging during peak hours — AM and PM rush on weekdays — when a wrong prediction means a dock is either empty or full at exactly the wrong time. I would recommend deploying this system only as a decision support tool rather than a fully automated rebalancing system, and only for high-demand stations where the model has enough training signal to be reliable. On the equity side, the spatial error analysis raises some real concerns. Prediction errors are not distributed evenly across Philadelphia — stations in lower-income neighborhoods tend to have less predictable demand patterns, which means the rebalancing algorithm informed by this model will consistently deprioritize those areas. This could worsen existing disparities in bike access, where communities that already have fewer transportation options end up with less reliable bike share service. To address this, I would recommend setting minimum service guarantees for lower-income stations that operate independently of model predictions, and regularly auditing errors by census tract to catch any systematic bias before it compounds over time. The model has a few meaningful limitations worth acknowledging. The biggest is that it cannot capture event-driven demand, like a Phillies game, a festival on the parkway, or a large convention will all cause spikes that look like noise to this model. It also assumes that the relationship between weather, time, and demand stays stable, which does not hold well across the summer-to-fall transition in Q3 where demand patterns shift fairly quickly in September. -------------------------------------------------
